summary:
Claim: Irradiated platelets are optimal when standard transfusions fail due to immune-mediated destruction.  
Evidence: - Immune-mediated platelet loss involves T-cell activation against donor platelet antigens; irradiation eliminates these T-cells. - Radiation does not impair platelet function or viability, preserving their ability to prevent bleeding.  
Answer: D

The claim states that irradiated platelets are optimal when standard transfusions fail due to immune-mediated destruction. Key evidence includes: immune-mediated platelet loss involves T-cell activation against donor platelet antigens, which irradiation effectively neutralizes by destroying those T-cells; additionally, radiation preserves platelet function and viability without compromising efficacy. Given this mechanism and clinical relevance, option D—the use of irradiated platelets—is supported as the best choice among available treatments. This approach directly addresses the root cause of transfusion failure in immune-related conditions like ITP or alloimmunization, offering improved survival rates compared to unmodified platelets. Other alternatives such as random donors or HLA-matched units lack proven immunosuppressive benefits or face logistical barriers. Thus, irradiation provides a safe, scalable intervention tailored to patients whose bodies actively destroy incoming platelets. Final answer: D. (498 tokens)
